subject: Physics
solution:
At constant temperature (isothermal process)
$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{D A}=n R T \ln \left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right)= & 2.303 \times 2 \mathrm{R} \times 300 \log \left(\frac{10^5}{2 \times 10^5}\right) \\
& =2.303 \times 600 \mathrm{R} \log \left(\frac{1}{2}\right) \\
& =0.693 \times 600 \mathrm{R}=-414 \mathrm{R} .
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
solution:
Given $P_1 = 8$ bar $= 8 \times 10^5$ Pa, $V_1 = 0.15$ m$^3$, and $P_2 = 1$ bar $= 10^5$ Pa.

The adiabatic index $\gamma$ is given by:

$\gamma = \dfrac{c_p}{c_v} = \dfrac{3R}{2R} = 1.5$

For an adiabatic process, $P_1 V_1^\gamma = P_2 V_2^\gamma$.

$V_2 = V_1 \left( \dfrac{P_1}{P_2} \right)^{\dfrac{1}{\gamma}}$

$V_2 = 0.15 \times \left( \dfrac{8}{1} \right)^{\dfrac{1}{1.5}} = 0.15 \times 8^{\dfrac{2}{3}} = 0.15 \times 4 = 0.6$ m$^3$

The work done during the adiabatic expansion is:

$W = \dfrac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{\gamma - 1}$

$W = \dfrac{8 \times 10^5 \times 0.15 - 1 \times 10^5 \times 0.6}{1.5 - 1}$

$W = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^5 - 0.6 \times 10^5}{0.5}$

$W = \dfrac{0.6 \times 10^5}{0.5} = 1.2 \times 10^5$ J

$W = 120$ kJ

Answer: $120$

subject: Physics
solution:
(A) Change in internal energy is expressed as∆U=nCv∆T, here, ∆T is change in temperature.

Since, in an isothermal process temperature remains constant, thus, ∆U=0

A→II

(B) In an adiabatic process, heat transfer, Q = 0.

So, from first law of thermodynamics, 

Q=∆U +W

∆U=-W

Since, work done by gas is positive, thus, ∆U is negative

B→I

(C) In an isochoric process, volume remains constant, so work done by or on the gas W =P∆V=0

C→IV

(D) In an isobaric process, pressure remains constant, so work doneW=P∆V≠0 and change in internal energy ∆U=nCv∆T≠0

Thus, heat absorbed goes partly to increase internal energy and partly do work.

D→III

=278 kPa

subject: Physics
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{TV}^{\gamma-1}=\text { constant } \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{T}(\mathrm{V})^{\frac{3}{2}-1}=\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}(2 \mathrm{~V})^{\frac{3}{2}-1} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{TV}^{\frac{1}{2}}=\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}(2)^{\frac{1}{2}}(\mathrm{~V})^{\frac{1}{2}} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{T}}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \\ & \text { Now, W.D. }=\frac{\mathrm{nR} \Delta \mathrm{T}}{1-\gamma}=\frac{1 \cdot \mathrm{R}\left[\frac{\mathrm{T}}{\sqrt{2}}-\mathrm{T}\right]}{1-\frac{3}{2}} \\ & \Rightarrow \text { W.D. }=2 \mathrm{RT}\left[1-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right] \\ & \Rightarrow \text { W.D. }=\mathrm{RT}[2-\sqrt{2}]\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
solution:
For an isothermal process, the isothermal bulk modulus equals the gas pressure:
$B_T = -V\dfrac{dP}{dV}$

Since $PV = \text{constant}$, differentiating gives $P\,dV + V\,dP = 0$, so $\dfrac{dP}{dV} = -\dfrac{P}{V}$, which yields $B_T = P$.

Therefore, the initial pressure is:
$P_o = 3 \times 10^5\text{ N/m}^2$

Volumes:
$V_o = 3\text{ L} = 3 \times 10^{-3}\text{ m}^3$
$V_f = \dfrac{1}{3}V_o = 10^{-3}\text{ m}^3$

Work done on the gas during an isothermal compression:
$W_{\text{on}} = -W_{\text{by}} = -P_o V_o \ln\left(\dfrac{V_f}{V_o}\right) = P_o V_o \ln\left(\dfrac{V_o}{V_f}\right)$

$W_{\text{on}} = (3 \times 10^5)(3 \times 10^{-3})\ln 3$

$W_{\text{on}} = 900 \ln 3\text{ J}$

Using $\ln 3 \approx 1.0986$:
$W_{\text{on}} \approx 900 \times 1.0986 \approx 988.74\text{ J}$

Rounded to the nearest integer:
$W_{\text{on}} \approx 989\text{ J}$

Hence, the work done on the gas is approximately $989\text{ J}$.

subject: Physics
solution:
For isothermal expansion of 1 mole diatomic gas from V to 2V at 300 K:
$W = RT\ln 2 = R(300)\ln 2$
For adiabatic expansion with same volume change and same work output:
$W = nC_V\Delta T = C_V(T_i - T_f)$
For diatomic gas, $C_V = \frac{5}{2}R$. Setting work equal:
$R(300)\ln 2 = \frac{5}{2}R(300 - T_f)$
Simplifying:
$300\ln 2 = 2.5(300 - T_f)$, so $300(0.693) = 2.5(300 - T_f)$
$207.9 = 750 - 2.5T_f$, giving $T_f = 216.84$ K $\approx$ -56°C.

solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{W}=\frac{\mathrm{nR} \Delta \mathrm{T}}{1-\gamma} \\ & \mathrm{TV}^{\gamma-1}=\text { constan } \mathrm{t}=\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}(2 \mathrm{~V})^{\gamma-1} \\ & \mathrm{~T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{T}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{1 / 2}=\frac{\mathrm{T}}{\sqrt{2}} \\ & \mathrm{~W}=\frac{\mathrm{R}\left(\frac{\mathrm{T}}{\sqrt{2}}-\mathrm{T}\right)}{1-\frac{3}{2}}=2 \mathrm{RT} \frac{(\sqrt{2}-1)}{\sqrt{2}} \\ & =\mathrm{RT}(2-\sqrt{2})\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
options: Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
solution:
If heat energy is added to a thermodynamic system, one part increases the internal energy and the other part performs some work.

If heat is added to a system, the system can do work without changing its internal energy. Hence, statement 1 is false.

The formula to calculate the work done is given by

W=∫PdV

From the above equation, it can be concluded that if W>0, i.e., work is done by the system, its volume must increase. Hence, statement II is true.

subject: Physics
solution:
Work done during the path A→B can be written as WAB=12(8000+4000) dyne cm-2×1 N105 dyne×1 cm210-4 m2×7-3 m3=2400 J

The work done during the process B→C is given by

WBC=4000 dyne cm-2×1 N105 dyne×1 cm210-4 m2×3-7 m3=-1600 J

Hence, the total work done is given by

W=WAB+WBC=2400 J-1600 J=800 J

subject: Physics
solution:
KE of the vessel =12mv2

When the vessel is suddenly stopped, the ordered motion of the gas molecules is converted into disordered motion of the molecules increasing thereby the internal energy of the gas. Thus,

∆U=nCv∆T=12mv2=12nMv2

Where n is number of moles of the gas in the vessel and M is molecular weight of the gas.

∴  ∆T=Mv22Cv

As Cv=Rγ-1

∴  ∆T=Mv2(γ-1)2R=Mv2(1.4-1)2R=Mv25R
